notes: Hand-rolled NEON intrinsic showdown matmul (subgame_vector_neon.rs) to try to close the 28% gap to the 300 ms @ 1000 iters target on river_canonical_spot. Replaced the i8->f32 widening + pos/neg accumulation in both showdown_matmul_rows and showdown_matmul_cols with NEON kernels (vld1q_s8 -> vmovl_s8/vmovl_s16 -> vcvtq_f32_s32, then vmaxq_f32 for the pos/neg split). Dispatch helpers (showdown_row_pos_neg, showdown_row_scatter_pos_neg) select NEON on aarch64 and keep a wide::f32x8 fallback for x86/scalar targets, both #[inline(always)]. Unit tests in subgame_vector_neon verify equivalence to scalar on 100 random inputs per kernel (dot, pos/neg, scatter) plus a tail-coverage sanity check. All solver-nlhe + solver-core tests pass, scripts/e2e.sh all 3 paths green. FINDING: NEON hand-rolling did NOT beat the pre-existing wide::f32x8 path on aarch64 because LLVM already compiled the `as f32` widening into SXTL/SXTL2 + SCVTF sequences for the f32x8-backed wide ops. Criterion baseline comparison on the same machine at the same run shows: river_canonical_spot 57.46 -> 57.65 ms (+0.3%, noise), river_degenerate_spot 18.49 -> 18.45 ms (-0.2%, noise), river_wet_board 59.48 -> 60.09 ms (+1.0%, noise). First integration attempt (with #[target_feature(enable="neon")]) regressed degenerate by 2x due to an inline barrier — removing the attribute restored parity. The kernel is correct and inlined, but the underlying i8->f32 widening was not the bottleneck the earlier hypothesis assumed. Machine numbers are ~40% higher than the A70 snapshot across ALL benches (flat_canonical went 467 -> 674 ms) indicating background CPU load at measurement time — the v0.1 A70 numbers are the canonical reference for target-hit checks. The NEON module remains in-tree as a documented dead-end: a working alternative implementation, useful as a reference point. The real bottleneck for the remaining 107 ms gap on canonical is not the widening — a profile is needed to identify what is (likely L1 pressure on the 1.76 MB showdown matrix, or regret-matching SIMD, or the tree walker).

river_canonical_spot.baseline: pre_neon (commit 9fac35e) on same machine, same session
river_canonical_spot.notes: AhKhQh2d4s, hero AA/KK/AKs, villain 22+/AJs+/KQs, pot 100, stack 500. No measurable change vs wide::f32x8. Machine was under ~40% broad load at bench time (Poker Panel + Claude + WindowServer active), so absolute number is ~40% higher than A70's 40.70 ms reference. On a quiescent machine the NEON and wide paths should both be in the 40-42 ms range.

river_degenerate_spot.baseline: pre_neon on same machine, same session
river_degenerate_spot.notes: 2c7d9hTsJs, hero AhKh vs villain AsAd, pot 1000, stack 0. Equivalent to wide path. First integration had #[target_feature(enable="neon")] which regressed this spot by +118% (40.45 ms) due to the inline barrier — removing the attribute restored parity. Machine-load-adjusted to A70's 13.02 ms reference.

river_wet_board.baseline: pre_neon on same machine, same session
river_wet_board.notes: JhTh9c8h7s, hero AA/AKs/QTs, villain 22+/AQs+. Within noise of wide path. Machine-load-adjusted to A70's 41.81 ms reference.

river_canonical_spot_flat.baseline: pre_neon on same machine, same session
river_canonical_spot_flat.notes: Scalar CfrPlusFlat path — does NOT use subgame_vector or the NEON kernel. The 16% improvement is machine-load drift between the pre_neon and post runs (the background load shifted), not a real change. Both numbers are ~40-70% higher than A70's 467.38 ms reference, same mechanism. Included as a regression guard only — a tree-walker change would move this number, and nothing in this commit touched the flat path.
